description: The provided image pixel is unreadable for scientific content, displaying only a publisher interface element labeled Check for updates. Based on the paper context and text extraction summary, the figure is inferred to present mechanical property data for near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steels. The chart likely displays engineering stress-strain curves comparing as-cast, annealed at 600 degrees Celsius for 3 hours, cold-rolled with 20 percent reduction, and cold-rolled plus annealed conditions across 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr alloys. Reported values include yield strengths ranging from 642 MPa to 967 MPa and ultimate tensile strengths up to 1270 MPa, with cold-rolled 3Cr and 6Cr strips showing significantly higher strength than Cr-free counterparts. The accompanying text reports that increasing Cr content raises delta-ferrite volume fraction from 3 percent to 24 percent, suppresses nano-scale kappa-carbide precipitation, and promotes high-density 111 slip bands with spacing decreasing from 371 nm to 45 nm. These mechanical data support the core conclusion that Cr addition enhances strain hardening and strengthens the steel while maintaining ductility through slip-band refinement.

The figure presents a combined mechanical-and-microstructural analysis of low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6 wt%) steel strips. The upper-left panel displays true stress–true strain curves (solid lines) with strain hardening rate plots (dashed lines, right y-axis) for the pre-cold-rolled condition, while the upper-right panel shows the corresponding curves after cold rolling with 20% reduction, revealing marked strength increases with Cr addition. The lower panels are bright-field TEM images of the 20% cold-rolled 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr specimens viewed near the [110] zone axis, showing planar {111} slip bands whose spacing decreases progressively with increasing Cr content. Selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) insets confirm the austenite matrix orientation. The data collectively show that higher Cr levels suppress nano-scale κ-carbide precipitation, promoting dense {111} slip bands that enhance strain hardening and tensile strength in the cold-rolled alloys.

Figure 1 presents X-ray diffraction patterns of low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr steel strips, comparing as-cast (a) and annealed (b) conditions for 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr alloys. In panel (a), all as-cast samples display face-centered-cubic austenite (γ, red indices) and body-centered-cubic δ-ferrite (δ, blue indices). A conspicuous double peak between 43.5° and 45.0° corresponds to overlapping (110)δ and (220)DO3 reflections; the area ratio of (110)δ to (220)DO3 decreases with increasing Cr content, indicating that Cr raises δ-ferrite fraction and fosters incipient DO3 ordering. Panel (b) shows that after annealing at 600 °C for 3 h, the double peak evolves into a dominant (220)DO3 single peak, signifying nearly complete transformation of δ-ferrite into the ordered DO3 phase (green indices), an effect enhanced by higher Cr. Austenite reflections persist across all compositions. These patterns demonstrate that Cr addition promotes δ-ferrite stabilization and its subsequent ordering to DO3 upon annealing, while correlating with suppressed nano-κ-carbide precipitation reported in the microstructural analysis.

This figure presents a set of six scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the duplex microstructure evolution in near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steel strips. Subfigures (a)-(c) display the as-cast condition for 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr alloys, respectively, while (d)-(f) show the corresponding annealed states (600 °C for 3 h). In all images, the γ-austenite matrix and δ-ferrite are labeled, with δ appearing as the brighter, irregularly shaped or blocky phase distributed within the darker γ matrix. A clear trend is visible: increasing Cr content from 0 to 6 wt% progressively raises the volume fraction of δ-ferrite and transforms its morphology from short rod-like or irregular shapes into more blocky forms. Additionally, comparison between as-cast and annealed pairs—(a) vs (d), (b) vs (e), and (c) vs (f)—reveals that annealing further increases δ-ferrite content. These micrographs directly demonstrate that Cr addition and subsequent annealing promote δ-ferrite formation, thereby altering the phase balance in the duplex microstructure.

Figure 3 presents the mechanical response of near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6 wt.%) low-density steel strips. Subfigures (a) and (b) show engineering stress-strain curves for as-cast and annealed conditions, respectively. The as-cast 0Cr alloy exhibits the highest yield strength with moderate elongation, whereas 3Cr shows the lowest yield strength but superior uniform elongation. After annealing, yield strengths increase markedly for all alloys, while ductility decreases slightly. Subfigures (c) and (d) overlay true stress-strain curves (solid lines) with strain hardening rate curves (dashed lines) for both conditions. Notably, the annealed 0Cr steel displays a pronounced double-hump feature in its strain hardening rate curve, which accompanying text attributes to sequential slip-band refinement and microband formation, whereas Cr-containing steels exhibit a gradually decreasing hardening rate. The caption and related discussion report that these trends arise because Cr addition suppresses nano-scale κ-carbide precipitation by expanding Al and C solid solubility, thereby altering the strain hardening mechanisms and strength-ductility balance.

Figure 4 presents TEM micrographs revealing nanoscale precipitated phases within γ-austenite and δ-ferrite of the as-cast Cr-free (0Cr) low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C steel strip. Panels (a)–(c) focus on γ-austenite: (a) is a bright-field (BF) image showing scattered contrast in the austenite matrix; (b) is a dark-field (DF) image using the (001) reflection, confirming that the nanoparticles are κ-carbides distributed throughout γ-austenite; and (c) is the selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) along the [110] zone axis exhibiting superlattice reflections that verify the ordered perovskite (Fe,Mn)3AlC κ-carbide phase with the orientation relationship [110]γ//[110]κ. Panels (d)–(f) target δ-ferrite: (d) is a BF image of irregularly shaped δ-ferrite; (e) is a DF image using the (002) superlattice reflection revealing the ordered DO3 phase; and (f) is the corresponding [110] SADP where diffuse superlattice spots confirm DO3 ordering in the ferrite. These micrographs establish that the as-cast 0Cr steel contains ~6 nm κ-carbides (volume fraction ~3%, lattice parameter 0.387 nm) in austenite and ~26 nm DO3 domains in δ-ferrite, serving as the baseline microstructure for evaluating Cr addition effects.

Figure 5 presents TEM micrographs and corresponding selected-area electron diffraction patterns (SADPs) from the as-cast 3Cr (Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-3Cr) low-density steel strip, characterizing nanoscale precipitates in both γ-austenite and δ-ferrite. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field (BF) TEM image of γ-austenite (labeled γ + κ) showing sparse nano-scale precipitates within the austenite matrix, with a scale bar of 500 nm. Subfigure (b) shows the SADP along the [110]γ zone axis, where indexed reflections correspond to the austenite matrix (hkl) and κ-carbide (hkl) precipitates, confirming their coexistence and orientation relationship. Subfigure (c) is a BF image of δ-ferrite (labeled δ + DO3), and (d) is a dark-field (DF) image formed using the (002) superlattice reflection of the ordered phase, revealing densely distributed DO3 ordered domains throughout the ferrite (scale bar 500 nm). Subfigure (e) presents the SADP along the [110]δ zone axis with reflections from both ferrite (hkl) and the DO3 superlattice (hkl). Collectively, these results demonstrate that in the as-cast 3Cr steel, κ-carbide precipitation in austenite is markedly reduced compared to the Cr-free alloy, while δ-ferrite contains nanoscale DO3 ordered precipitates, consistent with reported microstructural analyses.

This figure presents TEM characterization of the as-cast 6Cr low-density steel strip (Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-6Cr), revealing nanoscale precipitates in both γ-austenite and δ-ferrite phases. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field (BF) TEM micrograph of γ-austenite with a scale bar of 100 nm. Subfigure (b) shows the corresponding selected-area diffraction pattern (SADP) along the [011] zone axis, exhibiting reflections from both the austenite matrix and κ-carbide precipitates. Subfigures (c) and (d) focus on the δ-ferrite region: (c) is a BF image (500 nm scale bar) labeled δ+DO3, while (d) is a dark-field (DF) image formed using the (002) superlattice reflection of the ordered phase, highlighting the DO3 domain distribution. Subfigure (e) presents the SADP along the [110]δ zone axis from δ-ferrite, with indexed reflections distinguishing the ferrite matrix and the DO3 superlattice. Together, these micrographs provide direct crystallographic evidence that the as-cast 6Cr strip contains κ-carbides in the austenitic matrix and a DO3 ordered phase within δ-ferrite, supporting the conclusion that Cr addition alters phase stability and precipitation behavior in these lightweight steels.

Figure 7 presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6 wt%) steel strips, collected over a 2θ range of 30–100°. Subfigure (a) shows the CR20% cold-rolled condition, while subfigure (b) shows the CR20% + annealed condition, as stated in the caption. Each panel contains spectra for the 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr alloys stacked vertically. All patterns exhibit reflections indexed to γ-austenite (FCC, red labels: (111)γ, (200)γ, (220)γ, (311)γ, (222)γ) and δ-ferrite/DO3 (BCC, blue labels: (110)δ, (200)δ, (211)δ, (220)DO3). With increasing Cr content, the relative intensity of the δ-ferrite/DO3 reflections increases, indicating a higher ferrite fraction. In the CR20% state (a), the 6Cr alloy displays a double peak between approximately 43.5° and 45.0° corresponding to separate (110)δ and (220)DO3 reflections. After annealing (b), this feature merges into a single broad (110)δ/(220)DO3 peak, suggesting that annealing promotes the transformation of δ-ferrite to the ordered DO3 phase. The figure confirms retention of the duplex γ + δ/DO3 microstructure after cold rolling and annealing.

Figure 8 presents six scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs illustrating the microstructures of near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr steel strips subjected to 20% cold rolling (CR20%) and a subsequent annealing treatment (CR20% + annealed). Panels (a)-(c) correspond to the CR20% state of 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr alloys, respectively, while panels (d)-(f) represent the CR20% + annealed counterparts. The images are oriented with respect to the rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD), with a 20 μm scale bar. Each micrograph shows a two-phase structure consisting of a γ-austenite matrix and δ-ferrite, as indicated by arrows. A clear trend is visible: increasing Cr content leads to higher δ-ferrite volume fractions and a morphological transition from sparse irregular δ particles to more abundant, interconnected, and elongated δ-ferrite networks. The CR20% samples exhibit deformed microstructures, whereas the annealed samples display more equiaxed and recovered austenite grain structures. These observations support the conclusion that Cr addition promotes δ-ferrite formation and modifies duplex microstructure evolution during thermomechanical processing.

Figure 9 presents the mechanical response of near-rapidly solidified low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6 wt.%) steel strips after cold rolling with 20% reduction (CR20%) and subsequent annealing at 600 °C for 3 h (CR20% + annealed). Subfigures (a) and (b) display engineering stress-strain curves for CR20% and CR20% + annealed strips, respectively, where the 3Cr and 6Cr alloys consistently exhibit higher yield and ultimate tensile strengths than the 0Cr alloy. Subfigures (c) and (d) show true stress-strain curves (solid lines) together with strain hardening rate curves (dashed lines, dσ/dε). For CR20% strips in (c), Cr addition raises the strain hardening rate, which decreases gradually with increasing strain. After annealing in (d), the strain hardening rates of the 0Cr and 3Cr strips decrease markedly at the early stage compared with the CR20% condition. These trends support the conclusion that high-density slip bands induced by Cr addition dominate the strengthening of cold-rolled strips, while post-deformation annealing promotes κ-carbide precipitation at dislocation entanglements without substantially increasing yield strength because the pre-existing dense slip bands remain the primary strengthening factor.

Figure 10 presents bright-field TEM micrographs with inset selected-area diffraction patterns (SADP) along the [110] zone axis, revealing the deformation microstructures of γ-austenite in near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6 wt.%) steel strips after cold rolling with 20% reduction. Subfigures (a), (b), and (c) correspond to the 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr alloys, respectively, all exhibiting {111} planar slip bands. The images visually demonstrate a systematic decrease in slip band spacing with increasing Cr content: the 0Cr specimen shows widely spaced slip bands, whereas the 3Cr and 6Cr specimens display progressively higher slip band densities. Caption-reported measurements indicate average spacings of 371 ± 47 nm, 100 ± 15 nm, and 45 ± 13 nm for 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr, respectively. This Figure supports the core conclusion that decreasing slip band spacing with Cr addition is responsible for the enhanced reinforcement effect after cold rolling.

This figure presents TEM micrographs of γ-austenite in annealed (600 °C for 3 h) low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr steel strips (x = 0, 3, 6 wt.%). Panels (a), (c), and (e) are bright-field (BF) images showing the austenite matrix in the 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr strips, respectively, with insets displaying selected-area diffraction patterns (SADP) along the [110] zone axis where the circled (001) superlattice reflection confirms the presence of ordered κ-carbides. Panels (b), (d), and (f) are dark-field (DF) images formed using the (001) superlattice reflection to map the spatial distribution of nano-scale κ-carbides. The DF images reveal a marked decrease in both the number density and size of κ-carbides with increasing Cr content: the 0Cr strip shows a high density of bright κ-carbide particles, the 3Cr strip shows noticeably fewer, and the 6Cr strip shows virtually no discernible κ-carbide precipitation. The caption and accompanying text report that the annealed 0Cr strip contains κ-carbides of ~7 vol.% with an average size of 8 nm, while the 3Cr strip contains ~4 vol.% and 4 nm. These observations support the conclusion that Cr addition suppresses κ-carbide precipitation by expanding the solid solubility of Al and C, which reduces the driving force for κ-carbide formation and consequently lowers the yield strength of the annealed Cr-containing steels.

Figure 12 presents TEM bright-field and dark-field micrographs of γ-austenite in cold-rolled 20% plus annealed Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr steel strips with Cr contents of 0, 3, and 6 wt%. Subfigures (a), (c), and (e) show BF images of the austenite matrix in the 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr samples, respectively, with selected-area electron diffraction patterns along [110] in the insets where the (001) superlattice reflection is circled. Subfigures (b), (d), and (f) are DF images using the (001) reflection to reveal κ-carbides. Panels (b) and (d) show that κ-carbides, marked K, precipitate mainly at dislocation entanglements in the 0Cr and 3Cr strips. Panel (f) shows that in the 6Cr strip the κ-carbides are too small to observe. The trend demonstrates that Cr addition suppresses κ-carbide precipitation. These images support the conclusion that although annealing promotes κ-carbide formation, the pre-existing high-density slip bands and dislocations from cold rolling dominate strengthening, explaining why the yield strength does not significantly increase.

Figure 13 presents bright-field TEM micrographs comparing the deformation microstructures of annealed Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C steel strips without Cr (0Cr) and with 6 wt% Cr (6Cr) at a true tensile strain of 0.075. Panels (a) and (c) show {111} slip bands in austenite for 0Cr and 6Cr respectively, with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns along [110] confirming the FCC austenite structure; panel (a) additionally labels dislocation arrays. Panel (b) reveals a typical dislocation network structure in the 0Cr austenite, whereas panel (d) shows scattered and tangled dislocations between slip bands in the 6Cr steel. The micrographs demonstrate that Cr addition significantly reduces slip band spacing and increases slip band density, which promotes higher strain hardening rates by impeding dislocation glide. These features indicate a planar slip characteristic in both alloys, but the 6Cr steel exhibits more intense dislocation multiplication and entanglement compared to the dislocation network observed in 0Cr.

This figure presents four TEM micrographs (a–d) and associated selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns illustrating the post-tensile-fracture deformation microstructures of annealed low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr steel strips (0Cr and 6Cr). Subfigure (a) shows {111} planar slip bands in the 0Cr strip, with an inset SAED pattern along the [110] zone axis. Subfigure (b) reveals intersections between ferrite and microbands in the 0Cr strip (indicated by white arrows). Subfigure (c) captures a shearable grain boundary in the 6Cr strip, where slip bands extend across the boundary; the inset [110] SAED patterns from both sides confirm it is a low-angle grain boundary with a 2.56° misorientation. Subfigure (d) shows microbands in the 6Cr strip (marked by arrows). The images collectively demonstrate that dislocation entanglements occur at ferrite/austenite phase boundaries due to incompatible deformation, while slip bands can penetrate low-angle grain boundaries, leaving small steps. These observations support a double hardening mechanism involving slip-band refinement and microband formation in these duplex steels.
